mnemonics.title: Pipeline Stages Memory
mnemonics.content: IF ID EX MEM WB - Imagine Fun In DifferentMovies Everyday: Instruction Fetch, Instruction Decode, Execute, Memory, Write Back

mnemonics.title: Data Hazards Types
mnemonics.content: RAW = Read After Write (True dependency), WAR = Write After Read (Anti-dependency), WAW = Write After Write (Output dependency) - Remember: R-W-A顺序(sequence)

mnemonics.title: Speedup Formula
mnemonics.content: Speedup = n×k / (k+n-1) = Keep n multiplied by k, Divide by k plus n less one

acronyms.usage: Measures average number of clock cycles to execute one instruction; lower is better
True data dependency - instruction reads value written by previous instruction
Anti-dependency - instruction writes before a previous instruction reads
Output dependency - instruction writes before previous instruction writes
First stage of pipeline - fetches instruction from memory
Second stage - decodes instruction and reads registers
Fourth stage - accesses data memory if needed
Fifth stage - writes results back to register file
Throughput measure; higher indicates better performance

visualTips.visualization: Draw horizontal rows for each stage (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB), vertical columns for clock cycles. Show instruction boxes moving diagonally across stages. Bubbles appear as empty slots in the pipeline.
Plot a curve showing speedup approaching k (number of stages) as n increases. X-axis = number of instructions, Y-axis = speedup. Shows diminishing returns for small programs.
Structural hazard = two arrows pointing to one box. Data hazard = show dependency arrow between instructions. Control hazard = branch instruction showing uncertainty path.

analogies.analogy: Like a car assembly line where different workers do different tasks. While one worker installs engine, another paints body, another installs wheels. Each car takes same total time but line produces many cars per hour.
Like a relay race runner passing baton directly to next runner rather than going back to starting point first. Saves time by skipping unnecessary travel.
Like traffic jam on assembly line. When one station is blocked, all subsequent stations wait, reducing overall production rate.
Like at a fork in road where you must choose direction but don't know which is correct. You might go wrong way and have to backtrack, losing time.
